lessons.title: Introduction to LangChain and LLM Applications
lessons.content:
🔗 Introduction to LangChain and LLM Applications

📘 Lesson Overview: LangChain is a framework for building applications powered by Large Language Models (LLMs). It supplies the "plumbing" — prompts, chains, memory, retrieval, tools, and agents — so you orchestrate an LLM instead of just calling it once. This lesson explains what LangChain is, why it exists, its core building blocks, and a typical request flow.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain what LangChain is and the problem it solves.
  • Name the core components: models, prompts, chains, memory, retrieval, tools, agents.
  • Trace how a user request flows through a LangChain app.
  • Write and run a first LLMChain.
  • Decide when LangChain helps versus a raw API call.

1 — What LangChain Is

An LLM on its own is a single function: text in, text out. Real products need more — dynamic prompts, multi-step reasoning, memory of past turns, access to private documents, and the ability to call tools or APIs. LangChain is an orchestration framework that wires these pieces together with a consistent interface, so you can swap models or add capabilities without rewriting your app.

Concept

LangChain does not replace the model — it surrounds it. The LLM stays the reasoning engine; LangChain manages everything around the call: building the prompt, remembering context, fetching relevant data, and deciding what to do with the answer.

Rule: If your task is one prompt and one answer, call the API directly. Reach for LangChain when you need steps, state, data, or tools.

2 — Core Building Blocks

LangChain is modular. These are the pieces you compose:

CORE
Models
Wrappers over LLMs and chat models (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini, local) behind one interface.
CORE
Prompts
Reusable, parameterized templates that turn variables into model-ready text.
CORE
Chains
Linked steps where one component's output feeds the next — the heart of LangChain.
STATE
Memory
Carries conversation or task context across multiple calls.
DATA
Retrieval
Embeddings + vector stores fetch your private documents for grounded answers (RAG).
ACTION
Agents & Tools
Let the model choose and call tools (search, code, APIs) to act, not just answer.

3 — How a Request Flows

A typical LangChain app routes a user message through a small pipeline before (and sometimes after) the model:

LangChain Request Pipeline
Capture input

Take the user message and any app state.

Build the prompt

Fill a template with variables, history, and instructions.

Retrieve context

Optionally pull relevant chunks from a vector store (RAG).

Call the model

Send the assembled prompt to the LLM.

Parse / act

Structure the output, run tools, or update memory.

4 — Your First Chain

Install the packages, set your API key, then compose a prompt with a model. The modern style uses the LangChain Expression Language (LCEL) | pipe.

pip install langchain langchain-openai
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."   # Windows: setx OPENAI_API_KEY "sk-..."
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Explain {topic} to a beginner in 2 sentences."
)
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

chain = prompt | model           # LCEL: pipe prompt into model
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "vector embeddings"})
print(result.content)
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.chains import LLMChain
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Explain {topic} to a beginner in 2 sentences."
)
chain = LLMChain(llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"), prompt=prompt)
print(chain.invoke({"topic": "vector embeddings"})["text"])
Warning — keep keys out of code

Never hard-code API keys in source files. Use environment variables or a .env file loaded with python-dotenv, and add it to .gitignore.

5 — Raw API Call vs LangChain

Use LangChain when…
  • You need multi-step pipelines or branching.
  • You want memory across turns.
  • You ground answers in your own documents (RAG).
  • The model must call tools or APIs.
  • You expect to swap LLM providers later.
Skip it when…
  • One prompt, one response, no state.
  • A tiny script where a dependency is overkill.
  • You need byte-level control of one provider's payload.

6 — Quick Reference

ComponentJobTypical class
ModelRun the LLMChatOpenAI
PromptTemplate the inputChatPromptTemplate
ChainLink stepsprompt | model (LCEL)
MemoryKeep historyConversationBufferMemory
RetrieverFetch documentsvectorstore.as_retriever()
AgentChoose & call toolscreate_react_agent

7 — Practical Exercises

One-line summarizer

Build a chain that takes a text variable and returns a single-sentence summary. Print only the summary string.
Show Solution
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

chain = (
    ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Summarize in one sentence:\n\n{text}")
    | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
    | StrOutputParser()
)
print(chain.invoke({"text": "LangChain orchestrates LLM apps with prompts, chains, memory, retrieval and tools."}))

Two-step chain

Chain two prompts: first generate 3 keywords from a topic, then write a tweet using those keywords.
Show Solution
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
kw = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("List 3 keywords for: {topic}") | llm | StrOutputParser()
tweet = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Write a tweet using: {keywords}") | llm | StrOutputParser()

pipeline = {"keywords": kw} | tweet
print(pipeline.invoke({"topic": "retrieval augmented generation"}))
What you learned in this lesson
  • LangChain orchestrates LLM apps — it surrounds the model, not replaces it.
  • Core blocks: models, prompts, chains, memory, retrieval, tools/agents.
  • A request flows: input → prompt → (retrieval) → LLM → parse/act.
  • LCEL's | pipe is the modern way to build chains.
  • Use LangChain for steps/state/data/tools; skip it for one-shot calls.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a small "explainer" app: input a technical term, output a beginner explanation, a one-line analogy, and 3 follow-up questions — all from a single chain with a structured prompt.

options: It replaces the LLM with a faster, rule-based reasoning engine.
It compresses prompts so the model uses fewer tokens per call.
It orchestrates the pieces around the model — prompts, memory, retrieval, and tools — behind a consistent interface.
It fine-tunes the underlying model on your private data automatically.

lessons.title: Python and AI Foundations for LangChain
lessons.content:
🐍 Python and AI Foundations for LangChain

📘 Lesson Overview: LangChain is Python-first. Before building AI apps you need solid footing in the Python features they lean on — functions, classes, type hints, async, environment management, HTTP/APIs — plus the data stack (NumPy, Pandas) used to prepare inputs. This lesson is a focused refresher of exactly those foundations.
Learning Objectives
  • Use functions, type hints, and classes the way LangChain APIs expect.
  • Call REST APIs and parse JSON.
  • Manage secrets and virtual environments.
  • Use NumPy and Pandas for quick data prep.
  • Recognize where async matters in AI apps.

1 — Python Features You Will Actually Use

LangChain code is built on ordinary Python: functions that return values, classes you instantiate with keyword arguments, dictionaries for inputs, and increasingly type hints for structured outputs.

from typing import List
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Article(BaseModel):       # structured output schema
    title: str
    tags: List[str]

def make_prompt(topic: str, n: int = 3) -> str:
    return f"Write {n} tags for: {topic}"

print(make_prompt("vector search"))
Concept

LangChain uses Pydantic models to describe the shape of data you want back from an LLM. Learning Pydantic now pays off when you reach structured output and tool calling.

2 — APIs and JSON

Every hosted LLM is an HTTP API. Knowing how to call one and read JSON demystifies what LangChain does under the hood.

import requests

resp = requests.get("https://api.github.com/repos/langchain-ai/langchain")
data = resp.json()                 # dict
print(data["stargazers_count"])
Rule: Always check resp.status_code and wrap network calls in try/except. LLM APIs fail with rate limits and timeouts you must handle.

3 — Environments and Secrets

Create a venv

python -m venv .venv then activate it.

Install pinned deps

Keep a requirements.txt so builds are reproducible.

Load secrets

Put keys in .env, load with python-dotenv, gitignore the file.

from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os
load_dotenv()                       # reads .env
api_key = os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"]

4 — Data Stack: NumPy and Pandas

You will clean and shape data before feeding it to models, and you will work with embeddings — which are just NumPy vectors.

import numpy as np

a = np.array([0.1, 0.3, 0.5])
b = np.array([0.2, 0.1, 0.6])
cos = a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))   # cosine similarity
print(round(float(cos), 3))

Cosine similarity is the exact operation a vector database runs to find the closest documents. Here is how three documents might score against a query:

Doc A
0.92
Doc B
0.74
Doc C
0.41

5 — Sync vs Async

Async — use for apps
result = await chain.ainvoke(x)
# many requests in parallel
import asyncio
await asyncio.gather(*[chain.ainvoke(i) for i in items])
Sync — fine for scripts
result = chain.invoke(x)
# simple, blocks until done
for i in items:
    chain.invoke(i)     # serial, slower
Rule: LangChain mirrors every method with an async twin (invoke/ainvoke, stream/astream). Web backends (FastAPI, Streamlit callbacks) benefit from the async versions.

6 — Practical Exercises

Cosine similarity function

Write similarity(a, b) using NumPy that returns cosine similarity, and test it on two lists.
Show Solution
import numpy as np
def similarity(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))
print(round(similarity([1,0,1],[1,1,0]), 3))   # 0.5

Typed config with Pydantic

Define a Pydantic model LLMConfig with fields model: str and temperature: float = 0.0, then create an instance.
Show Solution
from pydantic import BaseModel
class LLMConfig(BaseModel):
    model: str
    temperature: float = 0.0
cfg = LLMConfig(model="gpt-4o-mini")
print(cfg.model_dump())
What you learned in this lesson
  • Functions, classes, and type hints are the Python base LangChain builds on.
  • Pydantic models describe structured LLM outputs.
  • Hosted LLMs are HTTP+JSON APIs; handle errors and timeouts.
  • Use venvs and .env for reproducible, secret-safe setups.
  • Embeddings are NumPy vectors compared by cosine similarity.
  • Prefer async methods (ainvoke) inside web apps.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Write a script that loads an API key from .env, calls any public REST API, loads the JSON into a Pandas DataFrame, and prints summary statistics. This rehearses the full input-prep path for an AI app.

To describe the shape of structured data you want back from an LLM.
To encrypt API keys stored in a .env file.

lessons.title: Natural Language Processing and LLM Fundamentals
lessons.content:
🧠 Natural Language Processing and LLM Fundamentals

📘 Lesson Overview: To use LLMs well you should understand what happens beneath the API: how text becomes tokens, how meaning becomes embeddings, and how the transformer with attention predicts the next token. This lesson builds that intuition with concrete examples.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain tokenization and why token count matters for cost and limits.
  • Describe embeddings and semantic similarity.
  • Outline the transformer architecture at a high level.
  • Explain attention in one sentence.
  • Connect these ideas to LangChain components.

1 — Tokenization

Models do not read characters or words — they read tokens, sub-word chunks. "tokenization" might split into token + ization. Token count drives context limits and cost, so it is worth measuring.

import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
text = "LangChain makes LLM apps composable."
ids = enc.encode(text)
print(len(ids), ids[:6])    # token count + first ids
Warning — context windows are finite

Every model has a maximum token window (prompt + answer). Exceed it and the call fails or silently truncates. This is the entire reason for text splitting and chunking, covered later.

2 — Embeddings

An embedding maps text to a vector of numbers so that similar meanings sit close together in space. This is what powers semantic search and RAG.

Concept

"car" and "automobile" produce nearby vectors even though they share no letters. Similarity is measured by the angle between vectors (cosine), not by string matching.

Typical dim
1536
OpenAI text-embedding-3-small
Distance
Cosine
angle between vectors
Stored in
Vector DB
FAISS, Chroma, Pinecone

3 — The Transformer and Attention

LLMs are transformers: stacks of layers that, for each token, weigh how much every other token matters — that weighting is attention. The model then predicts the most likely next token, appends it, and repeats.

Next-Token Generation
Memory Aid — attention in one line

"When predicting the next word, look hardest at the words that matter most." That selective looking is attention.

4 — Sampling: Temperature

The model outputs a probability for every possible next token. Temperature controls how randomly it picks: low = focused and repeatable, high = creative and varied.

TemperatureBehaviorUse for
0.0Deterministic, focusedExtraction, classification, code
0.7BalancedChat, general writing
1.0+Creative, variedBrainstorming, ideation

5 — How This Maps to LangChain

Tokens → limits

Drive the text splitters and chunk sizes you will configure.

Embeddings → retrieval

Power vector stores and RAG retrievers.

Transformer → the model

Is the ChatOpenAI/ChatAnthropic object you call.

Temperature → config

A parameter you set when constructing the model.

6 — Practical Exercises

Count tokens and estimate cost

Use tiktoken to count tokens in a paragraph, then estimate cost at $0.15 per 1M input tokens.
Show Solution
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
text = "Your paragraph here ..."
n = len(enc.encode(text))
print(n, "tokens", f"${n/1_000_000*0.15:.6f}")

Nearest neighbor by cosine

Given a query vector and three document vectors, print which document is most similar.
Show Solution
import numpy as np
def cos(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
q = [0.1, 0.9, 0.2]
docs = {"A": [0.1, 0.8, 0.3], "B": [0.9, 0.1, 0.0], "C": [0.2, 0.2, 0.9]}
best = max(docs, key=lambda k: cos(q, docs[k]))
print("Most similar:", best)
What you learned in this lesson
  • Text is processed as tokens; token count drives limits and cost.
  • Embeddings turn meaning into vectors compared by cosine similarity.
  • Transformers use attention to weigh tokens and predict the next one.
  • Temperature trades determinism for creativity.
  • Each idea maps directly to a LangChain component you will use.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Take three sentences, embed them with any embedding model (or mock vectors), build a 3×3 cosine-similarity matrix, and identify the most and least related pair.

lessons.title: Large Language Models and Generative AI
lessons.content:
🤖 Large Language Models and Generative AI

📘 Lesson Overview: LangChain is provider-agnostic — it can drive GPT, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA, Mistral, and local models through one interface. This lesson surveys the major LLM families, contrasts closed vs open models, explains context windows and cost, and shows how to call any of them from LangChain.
Learning Objectives
  • Identify the major LLM families and who builds them.
  • Compare closed (API) vs open-weight models.
  • Explain context window, cost, and latency trade-offs.
  • Swap models in LangChain with a one-line change.
  • Pick a model for a given task.

1 — The Major Families

OPENAI
GPT-4o / o-series
Strong general reasoning and tool use; the default many apps start with.
ANTHROPIC
Claude
Long context, careful instruction-following, strong coding and analysis.
GOOGLE
Gemini
Natively multimodal with very large context windows.
OPEN
LLaMA · Mistral
Open-weight models you can self-host for privacy and cost control.

2 — Closed vs Open

Closed / API (GPT, Claude, Gemini)
  • Best-in-class quality, zero ops.
  • Pay per token; data leaves your network.
  • Fast to start, easy to scale.
Open weights (LLaMA, Mistral)
  • Run on your hardware; full data control.
  • Needs GPUs and MLOps effort.
  • Lower per-call cost at scale.
Rule: Prototype on a hosted API. Move to open weights only when privacy, cost-at-scale, or customization demands it.

3 — Context Windows & Cost

The context window is how many tokens fit in one call. Bigger windows let you stuff more documents in, but cost and latency grow with tokens used.

Small / cheap
~8K
classify, extract
Standard
~128K
chat, RAG
Long context
1M+
whole codebases
Warning — big window is not free

A 1M-token window does not mean you should fill it. You pay for every token sent. Retrieval (RAG) that sends only the relevant chunks is usually cheaper and more accurate than dumping everything.

4 — One Interface, Many Models

LangChain's chat-model classes share the same methods, so switching providers is a one-line change.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
model = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-sonnet-latest")
from langchain_google_genai import ChatGoogleGenerativeAI
model = ChatGoogleGenerativeAI(model="gemini-1.5-pro")
from langchain_ollama import ChatOllama
model = ChatOllama(model="llama3.1")   # runs on your machine

Everything downstream — prompt | model | parser — stays identical.

5 — Choosing a Model

TaskPickWhy
Classify / extractSmall fast modelCheap, deterministic at temp 0
Chat assistantMid flagshipQuality + reasonable cost
Long documentsLong-context modelFits more in one call
Private dataOpen weights, self-hostData never leaves
MultimodalGemini / GPT-4oImage + text natively

6 — Practical Exercises

Provider swap

Write a chain with a fixed prompt + parser, then run it once with an OpenAI model and once with a Claude model, changing only one line.
Show Solution
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("One fun fact about {x}.")
parser = StrOutputParser()
for model in [ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"), ChatAnthropic(model="claude-3-5-sonnet-latest")]:
    print((prompt | model | parser).invoke({"x": "octopuses"}))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Major families: GPT, Claude, Gemini (closed); LLaMA, Mistral (open).
  • Closed = quality + zero ops; open = control + cost-at-scale.
  • Context window sets how much fits per call; tokens drive cost.
  • LangChain swaps models with a one-line change.
  • Match the model to the task, not the hype.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a tiny "model comparison" script: same prompt, three providers, print each answer plus its latency. Note quality and speed differences.

options: They automatically fine-tune the model on your data
Switching providers becomes a one-line change
They remove the need for API keys
questions.text: According to the lesson, what is the main advantage of LangChain's chat-model classes sharing the same methods?

options: Always self-host open weights from day one
Prototype on a hosted API; move to open weights only when privacy, cost-at-scale, or customization demands it
Never use hosted APIs because data leaves your network
Use open weights only for multimodal tasks
questions.text: What does the lesson recommend as the general rule for prototyping vs open-weight models?

options: You pay for every token sent, so filling it is often costlier and less accurate than RAG
Large windows only work with open-weight models
The model ignores tokens beyond the first 8K
questions.text: The lesson warns that a very large context window (e.g. 1M tokens) is not free. Why?

lessons.title: Setting Up LangChain Development Environment
lessons.content:
🛠️ Setting Up LangChain Development Environment

📘 Lesson Overview: A clean, reproducible environment prevents most beginner pain. This lesson sets up Python with a virtual environment, installs LangChain and a provider package, manages API keys safely, and verifies the install with a smoke-test script.
Learning Objectives
  • Create and activate a virtual environment.
  • Install LangChain and provider packages.
  • Store API keys safely with .env.
  • Verify the setup with a smoke test.
  • Lay out a sensible project structure.

1 — Environment Setup

Create venv

Isolates dependencies per project.

Activate

Windows vs macOS/Linux differ slightly.

Install

LangChain core + one provider + dotenv.

Configure keys

Put secrets in .env, never in code.

python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
pip install langchain langchain-openai python-dotenv
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install langchain langchain-openai python-dotenv

2 — API Keys with .env

Create a .env file in your project root and load it at runtime.

# .env  (add this file to .gitignore!)
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()        # now os.environ has your keys
# LangChain provider classes read these automatically
Warning — never commit secrets

Add .env and .venv/ to .gitignore before your first commit. A leaked key can be abused within minutes.

3 — Smoke Test

Confirm everything works with the smallest possible call.

from dotenv import load_dotenv
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
load_dotenv()
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
print(model.invoke("Reply with the single word: ready").content)
$ python smoke_test.py ready

4 — Project Structure

PathPurpose
.venv/Virtual environment (gitignored)
.envSecrets (gitignored)
requirements.txtPinned dependencies
app.pyEntry point
chains/Reusable chains
data/Documents for RAG
Rule: Run pip freeze > requirements.txt whenever you add a package, so teammates and servers reproduce your exact setup.

5 — Practical Exercises

Reproducible setup

From scratch, create a venv, install LangChain + a provider, add a .env, write and run a smoke test that prints the model's reply.
Show Solution
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install langchain langchain-openai python-dotenv
printf "OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...\n" > .env
printf ".env\n.venv/\n" > .gitignore
python smoke_test.py    # uses the script from section 3
What you learned in this lesson
  • Use a venv per project to isolate dependencies.
  • Install LangChain core + a provider package + dotenv.
  • Keep keys in .env; gitignore secrets and the venv.
  • Verify with a one-line smoke test before building.
  • Pin dependencies in requirements.txt.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Turn your setup into a reusable template repo: include .gitignore, requirements.txt, a .env.example, and a smoke test. Clone it for every new LangChain project.

options: It encrypts your API keys automatically
It is required to call any hosted LLM
questions.text: Why does the lesson recommend creating a virtual environment (venv) for each project?

options: Hard-coded at the top of app.py
In a .env file that is added to .gitignore
In the prompt template as a variable
questions.text: According to the lesson, where should API keys be stored?
What command does the lesson suggest running whenever you add a package, so setups are reproducible?

lessons.title: Understanding LangChain Architecture
lessons.content:
🏗️ Understanding LangChain Architecture

📘 Lesson Overview: LangChain is built on one big idea — the Runnable. Every component (prompt, model, parser, retriever) is a Runnable with the same methods, so you compose them with the | pipe. This lesson maps the full architecture and the role of each layer.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain the Runnable interface and why it unifies LangChain.
  • Describe each architectural layer and its job.
  • Compose components with LCEL.
  • Know where memory, retrieval, and tools plug in.

1 — Everything Is a Runnable

A Runnable is any object with invoke, stream, and batch (plus async twins). Because prompts, models, and parsers all implement it, the | operator chains them into a pipeline.

Concept

prompt | model | parser reads left to right: the prompt's output is the model's input, and the model's output is the parser's input. The whole pipeline is itself a Runnable you can invoke, stream, or batch.

chain = prompt | model | parser
chain.invoke({"topic": "vectors"})   # run once
chain.batch([{"topic": "a"}, {"topic": "b"}])   # many
for tok in chain.stream({"topic": "x"}):         # stream
    print(tok, end="")

2 — The Layers

LangChain Architecture
LayerResponsibility
PromptTurn variables + history into model input
ModelRun the LLM, return a message
Output parserConvert text into strings, JSON, or objects
RetrieverFetch relevant documents (RAG)
MemoryPersist conversation/task state
Tools / AgentsLet the model act on the world

3 — Composing with RunnableParallel

Run several Runnables on the same input and collect a dict of results — handy for building prompt context.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel, RunnablePassthrough

setup = RunnableParallel(
    context=retriever,                 # fetch docs
    question=RunnablePassthrough(),     # pass input through
)
rag_chain = setup | prompt | model | parser

4 — Practical Exercises

Three-stage Runnable

Compose prompt | model | StrOutputParser() and call batch with two inputs in one go.
Show Solution
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Define {term} in 6 words.") | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini") | StrOutputParser()
print(chain.batch([{"term": "embedding"}, {"term": "agent"}]))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Every component is a Runnable with invoke/stream/batch.
  • The | pipe composes Runnables into chains.
  • Layers: prompt, model, parser, retriever, memory, tools.
  • RunnableParallel assembles context dictionaries.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Diagram your own future app as Runnables: which pieces are prompt, model, retriever, memory, tools? Then build the skeleton with RunnablePassthrough placeholders.

options: The Runnable — every component shares invoke, stream, and batch
The Embedding — every component is a vector
The Agent — every component can call tools
The Token — every component counts tokens
questions.text: The lesson says LangChain is built on one big idea. What is it?

options: Right to left, parser output feeds the prompt
Left to right, each component's output becomes the next one's input
All three run in parallel and results are merged
The model runs first, then the prompt reshapes it
questions.text: In the pipeline 'prompt | model | parser', how does data flow?

options: Runs the same Runnable multiple times for retries
Runs several Runnables on the same input and collects a dict of results
Splits a document into parallel chunks
Streams tokens to multiple clients at once
questions.text: What does RunnableParallel do, according to the lesson?

lessons.title: Prompt Templates and Prompt Engineering
lessons.content:
✍️ Prompt Templates and Prompt Engineering

📘 Lesson Overview: The prompt is your main control surface. LangChain turns prompts into reusable templates with variables, roles, examples, and structured output. This lesson covers prompt templates plus the core engineering techniques: clear instructions, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured output.
Learning Objectives
  • Build PromptTemplate and ChatPromptTemplate.
  • Use system vs human messages.
  • Apply few-shot and chain-of-thought prompting.
  • Force structured output with a schema.

1 — Prompt Templates

A template holds placeholders that you fill at call time, so the same prompt serves many inputs.

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a concise {domain} tutor."),
    ("human", "Explain {concept} with one example."),
])
msgs = prompt.invoke({"domain": "Python", "concept": "list comprehension"})
Rule: Put the role and constraints in the system message, the task and data in the human message. Keep them separate and explicit.

2 — Good vs Vague Prompts

Specific
Summarize the text below in exactly
3 bullet points, <=12 words each,
no jargon. Text: ...
Vague
Summarize this.

Constraints (count, length, format, audience) make outputs predictable and easy to parse.

3 — Few-Shot Prompting

Show the model a couple of examples so it copies the pattern.

from langchain_core.prompts import FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate, ChatPromptTemplate

examples = [
    {"word": "happy", "antonym": "sad"},
    {"word": "fast",  "antonym": "slow"},
]
ex_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([("human", "{word}"), ("ai", "{antonym}")])
few_shot = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(examples=examples, example_prompt=ex_prompt)
final = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([few_shot, ("human", "{word}")])

4 — Chain-of-Thought & Structured Output

For reasoning tasks, ask the model to think step by step. For machine-readable results, bind a schema.

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Solve step by step, then give the final answer on a new line.\n{problem}"
)
from pydantic import BaseModel
class Sentiment(BaseModel):
    label: str
    score: float
structured = model.with_structured_output(Sentiment)
print(structured.invoke("I love this!"))   # Sentiment(label='positive', score=...)
Warning — do not parse free text by hand

If you need JSON or fields, use with_structured_output or an output parser. Regex over free-form model text breaks the moment the wording changes.

5 — Technique Cheat Sheet

TechniqueWhen
Clear constraintsAlways
Few-shotSpecific format or style needed
Chain-of-thoughtMath, logic, multi-step reasoning
Structured outputOutput feeds code/DB
Role / personaSet tone and expertise

6 — Practical Exercises

Sentiment extractor

Use with_structured_output to return a Pydantic Sentiment(label, score) for any input sentence.
Show Solution
from pydantic import BaseModel
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
class Sentiment(BaseModel):
    label: str
    score: float
m = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini").with_structured_output(Sentiment)
print(m.invoke("This product is okay, nothing special."))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Templates make prompts reusable with variables and roles.
  • System = role/constraints; human = task/data.
  • Few-shot teaches a pattern; chain-of-thought aids reasoning.
  • Use structured output for machine-readable results.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a "review analyzer" prompt that returns structured output: sentiment, a one-line summary, and up to 3 topic tags. Test it on five different reviews.

options: Role and constraints in the system message; task and data in the human message
The data in the system message; the role in the human message
Everything should go in a single human message
Few-shot examples in the system message only
questions.text: According to the lesson's rule, what belongs in the system message vs the human message?

options: Only for math and multi-step logic
When a specific output format or style is needed
Only when the output feeds a database
questions.text: When is few-shot prompting recommended in the technique cheat sheet?

options: A regular expression over the raw output
questions.text: The lesson warns against parsing free-form model text by hand. What should you use instead for machine-readable results?

lessons.title: Chains and Sequential Workflows
lessons.content:
⛓️ Chains and Sequential Workflows

📘 Lesson Overview: A chain links steps so output flows into input. With LCEL you build sequential chains (|), parallel branches (RunnableParallel), and conditional routing (RunnableBranch). This lesson covers all three plus data transformations between steps.
Learning Objectives
  • Build sequential chains with LCEL.
  • Run steps in parallel and merge results.
  • Route inputs conditionally with a branch.
  • Transform data between steps.

1 — Sequential Chains

The output of one step becomes the input of the next. Wrap a step's output in a dict to feed named variables downstream.

from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

outline = (prompt_outline | model | StrOutputParser())
draft   = (prompt_draft   | model | StrOutputParser())

chain = {"outline": outline} | draft     # outline feeds the draft prompt
print(chain.invoke({"topic": "tides"}))
Sequential vs Parallel

2 — Parallel Branches

Compute several things from one input at once, then combine.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel

analyze = RunnableParallel(
    summary=summary_chain,
    keywords=keyword_chain,
    sentiment=sentiment_chain,
)
result = analyze.invoke({"text": "..."})   # dict with all three

3 — Conditional Routing

Pick a sub-chain based on the input — e.g., route math questions to a calculator chain and everything else to a chat chain.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableBranch

router = RunnableBranch(
    (lambda x: x["kind"] == "math", math_chain),
    (lambda x: x["kind"] == "code", code_chain),
    default_chain,                              # fallback
)
Rule: Keep each branch a self-contained chain. Routing logic should only decide which chain runs, not do the work itself.

4 — Transformations Between Steps

Use RunnableLambda to reshape data mid-pipeline.

from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

upper = RunnableLambda(lambda s: s.strip().upper())
chain = prompt | model | StrOutputParser() | upper

5 — Practical Exercises

Parallel analyzer

Build a RunnableParallel that returns both a summary and 3 keywords for a piece of text in one call.
Show Solution
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
S = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Summarize in one sentence: {text}") | llm | StrOutputParser()
K = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("3 keywords for: {text}") | llm | StrOutputParser()
print(RunnableParallel(summary=S, keywords=K).invoke({"text": "LangChain composes LLM steps."}))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Sequential chains pass output to input via the | pipe.
  • RunnableParallel runs steps at once and returns a dict.
  • RunnableBranch routes inputs to the right sub-chain.
  • RunnableLambda transforms data between steps.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a router that sends questions to a "concise" chain or a "detailed" chain based on a style flag, with a default for unknown styles.

options: By wrapping the output in a dict, e.g. {'outline': outline} | draft
By raising an exception the next step catches
By storing it in a global variable
By re-embedding it as a vector
questions.text: In a sequential chain, how is one step's output passed to the next named variable downstream?

options: Runs every branch and averages the results
Routes an input to the right sub-chain based on a condition, with a default fallback
questions.text: What does RunnableBranch do in the lesson?
Which component does the lesson use to transform or reshape data between steps in a pipeline?

lessons.title: LLM Integration and API Management
lessons.content:
🔌 LLM Integration and API Management

📘 Lesson Overview: Production AI apps must integrate multiple providers and handle the messy reality of APIs: keys, rate limits, retries, timeouts, streaming, and cost tracking. This lesson shows how LangChain unifies providers and how to make calls robust.
Learning Objectives
  • Integrate OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Cohere, and Hugging Face.
  • Configure keys, timeouts, and retries.
  • Stream responses token by token.
  • Track and control token cost.

1 — Provider Packages

PIP
langchain-openai
GPT chat + embeddings.
PIP
langchain-anthropic
Claude chat models.
PIP
langchain-google-genai
Gemini multimodal.
PIP
langchain-huggingface
Open models, local or hub.

2 — Robust Configuration

Set timeouts and automatic retries so transient failures do not crash your app.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

model = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    timeout=30,        # seconds
    max_retries=3,     # retry on rate limit / network errors
    temperature=0,
)
Warning — handle rate limits

Providers throttle requests. Rely on max_retries with exponential backoff (built in), and for bulk work use batch with a concurrency limit rather than a tight loop.

3 — Streaming

Stream tokens to show output as it is generated — essential for chat UIs.

for chunk in model.stream("Write a haiku about the sea"):
    print(chunk.content, end="", flush=True)

4 — Cost Awareness

Cost is roughly tokens × price. Track usage and prefer smaller models where quality allows.

Small model
$0.15
per 1M input tokens
vs
Flagship
$5.00
per 1M input tokens
Rule: Use a small model for routing, extraction, and classification; reserve the flagship for the hard reasoning step. This "model tiering" can cut cost by 10x.

5 — Env Var Reference

ProviderEnv var
OpenAIOPENAI_API_KEY
AnthropicANTHROPIC_API_KEY
GoogleGOOGLE_API_KEY
CohereCOHERE_API_KEY
Hugging FaceHUGGINGFACEHUB_API_TOKEN

6 — Practical Exercises

Resilient streaming call

Configure a model with a 20s timeout and 2 retries, then stream a short story to the console.
Show Solution
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
m = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", timeout=20, max_retries=2)
for c in m.stream("Tell a 3-sentence story about a brave robot."):
    print(c.content, end="", flush=True)
What you learned in this lesson
  • Each provider has its own langchain-* package, one interface.
  • Set timeout and max_retries for resilience.
  • Stream tokens for responsive chat UIs.
  • Track cost; tier small vs flagship models to save money.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a wrapper function ask(prompt, tier="small") that selects a cheap or flagship model, applies retries, and returns the answer plus token usage.

options: Set timeout and max_retries on the model
Send every request twice in a tight loop
questions.text: What does the lesson recommend to make LLM calls resilient to transient failures?

options: Using a small model for routing/extraction/classification and reserving the flagship for hard reasoning, cutting cost
Stacking multiple models to increase context length
Running the same model at several temperatures
questions.text: What is the purpose of 'model tiering' as described in the lesson?

options: It reduces the total token cost of a call
It shows output as it is generated, essential for chat UIs
It lets you skip setting an API key
questions.text: Why is streaming tokens useful, according to the lesson?

lessons.title: Memory Systems and Conversational AI
lessons.content:
💬 Memory Systems and Conversational AI

📘 Lesson Overview: LLM calls are stateless — the model forgets the previous turn. Memory feeds prior messages back into each call so a conversation makes sense. This lesson covers message history, buffer vs summary vs windowed memory, and the modern RunnableWithMessageHistory wrapper.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain why LLM calls are stateless.
  • Attach history with RunnableWithMessageHistory.
  • Choose buffer, window, or summary memory.
  • Keep multi-user sessions separate.

1 — Why Memory Is Needed

Each API call only sees what you send. To answer "what did I just ask?", you must resend earlier turns. Memory automates collecting and injecting that history.

Conversation Loop

2 — History with RunnableWithMessageHistory

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, MessagesPlaceholder
from langchain_core.chat_history import InMemoryChatMessageHistory
from langchain_core.runnables.history import RunnableWithMessageHistory

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful assistant."),
    MessagesPlaceholder("history"),
    ("human", "{input}"),
])
chain = prompt | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

store = {}
def get_history(session_id):
    return store.setdefault(session_id, InMemoryChatMessageHistory())

chat = RunnableWithMessageHistory(chain, get_history,
        input_messages_key="input", history_messages_key="history")

cfg = {"configurable": {"session_id": "user-1"}}
print(chat.invoke({"input": "My name is Sid."}, cfg).content)
print(chat.invoke({"input": "What is my name?"}, cfg).content)   # remembers
Rule: The session_id keeps users separate. Use a real store (Redis, a DB) in production instead of the in-memory dict.

3 — Memory Types

Buffer / Window
  • Keep the last N messages verbatim.
  • Simple, exact, cheap for short chats.
  • Grows with conversation length.
Summary
  • Compress old turns into a running summary.
  • Constant size for long chats.
  • Loses fine detail; costs an extra LLM call.
NeedUse
Short chatFull buffer
Long chat, token budgetWindow (last N) or summary
Recall facts across sessionsVector memory (embed + retrieve)

4 — Practical Exercises

Two-turn memory

Build a chat that remembers a fact from turn 1 and uses it in turn 2, keyed by session id.
Show Solution
# Reuse the section-2 setup, then:
cfg = {"configurable": {"session_id": "demo"}}
chat.invoke({"input": "I like the color teal."}, cfg)
print(chat.invoke({"input": "What color do I like?"}, cfg).content)
What you learned in this lesson
  • LLM calls are stateless; memory resends prior turns.
  • RunnableWithMessageHistory wires history into a chain.
  • session_id isolates users.
  • Buffer/window keep detail; summary keeps size constant.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Add windowed memory (last 6 messages) to a chatbot and confirm older turns drop off while recent context stays.

options: Because LLM calls are stateless and each call only sees what you send
Because embeddings expire after each turn
Because the model retrains itself on every message
questions.text: Why is memory needed for conversations, according to the lesson?
In the lesson's setup, what keeps multiple users' conversations separate?

options: It keeps the last N messages verbatim
It compresses old turns into a running summary, keeping size constant but losing fine detail
It stores messages as vectors only
It disables memory after a fixed number of turns
questions.text: How does summary memory differ from buffer/window memory?

lessons.title: Document Loaders and Data Ingestion
lessons.content:
📂 Document Loaders and Data Ingestion

📘 Lesson Overview: Before an LLM can reason over your data, you must load it into LangChain's Document objects. Loaders read PDFs, Word, CSV, web pages, and more into a uniform shape — text plus metadata. This is step one of every RAG pipeline.
Learning Objectives
  • Describe the Document object.
  • Load PDFs, CSVs, text, and web pages.
  • Use and preserve metadata.
  • Pick the right loader per source.

1 — The Document Object

Every loader returns a list of Documents. Each has page_content (the text) and metadata (source, page, etc.).

Concept

Uniform Document shape is what lets the rest of the pipeline — splitters, embedders, vector stores — work the same regardless of where the data came from.

from langchain_core.documents import Document
doc = Document(page_content="Hello", metadata={"source": "demo.txt", "page": 1})
print(doc.page_content, doc.metadata)

2 — Common Loaders

PDF
PyPDFLoader
One Document per page, with page numbers in metadata.
CSV
CSVLoader
One Document per row; columns become text.
WEB
WebBaseLoader
Fetch and clean a web page into text.
TXT
TextLoader
Plain text files; the simplest loader.
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
docs = PyPDFLoader("report.pdf").load()
print(len(docs), docs[0].metadata["page"])
from langchain_community.document_loaders import CSVLoader
docs = CSVLoader("sales.csv").load()
print(docs[0].page_content)
from langchain_community.document_loaders import WebBaseLoader
docs = WebBaseLoader("https://example.com").load()
print(docs[0].page_content[:200])
Warning — install extras

Many loaders need extra packages (e.g. pypdf for PDFs, beautifulsoup4 for web). Install them or the loader raises an ImportError.

3 — Keep Metadata

Metadata (source, page, URL) lets you cite where an answer came from. Preserve it through the whole pipeline.

SourceLoaderExtra
PDFPyPDFLoaderpypdf
WordDocx2txtLoaderdocx2txt
CSVCSVLoader—
WebWebBaseLoaderbeautifulsoup4
FolderDirectoryLoaderper-type

4 — Practical Exercises

Load and inspect

Load any PDF, print the number of pages and the metadata of the first page.
Show Solution
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
docs = PyPDFLoader("any.pdf").load()
print("pages:", len(docs))
print("meta:", docs[0].metadata)
What you learned in this lesson
  • Loaders produce Documents with text + metadata.
  • One loader per source type; many need extra packages.
  • Preserve metadata to cite sources later.
  • Loading is step one of every RAG pipeline.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Use DirectoryLoader to ingest a folder of mixed files, then print a count of Documents grouped by source extension.

options: A vector and a similarity score
page_content (the text) and metadata (source, page, etc.)
An embedding and a token count
questions.text: What two things does every LangChain Document object contain?

options: It lets you cite where an answer came from
It reduces the number of tokens sent
It is required to activate streaming

lessons.title: Text Splitting and Document Chunking
lessons.content:
✂️ Text Splitting and Document Chunking

📘 Lesson Overview: Documents are too big to embed or send whole, so you split them into chunks. Chunk size and overlap directly control retrieval quality. This lesson covers recursive, token-based, and semantic splitting, and how to tune them.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain why chunking is required.
  • Use RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.
  • Tune chunk_size and chunk_overlap.
  • Choose character vs token vs semantic splitting.

1 — Why Chunk

Embeddings work best on focused passages, and context windows are finite. Splitting keeps each chunk small enough to embed and relevant enough to retrieve.

Ingestion Pipeline

2 — Recursive Splitting

The default splitter tries to break on paragraphs, then lines, then words — keeping chunks coherent.

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=1000,        # characters per chunk
    chunk_overlap=150,      # carry-over to keep context
)
chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
print(len(chunks), len(chunks[0].page_content))

3 — Tuning Size & Overlap

Small chunks = precise but fragmented; large chunks = more context but noisier and costlier. Overlap prevents cutting an idea in half.

Small (300)
precise
Medium (1000)
balanced
Large (3000)
noisy
Rule: Start at chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=100–200, then adjust based on retrieval quality. There is no universal best — it depends on your documents.

4 — Character vs Token vs Semantic

SplitterSplits byUse when
Recursive characterSeparators + lengthDefault, general text
TokenModel tokensHard token budgets
Markdown / codeStructureDocs, source files
SemanticMeaning shiftsHighest quality, slower

5 — Practical Exercises

Split and count

Load a long text file, split with size 800 / overlap 120, and print how many chunks result.
Show Solution
from langchain_community.document_loaders import TextLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
docs = TextLoader("big.txt").load()
chunks = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800, chunk_overlap=120).split_documents(docs)
print("chunks:", len(chunks))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Chunking fits documents into embeddings and context windows.
  • RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter keeps chunks coherent.
  • Size and overlap trade precision against context.
  • Token/semantic splitters exist for special needs.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Split the same document at three sizes (300/1000/3000) and eyeball which chunks read as self-contained ideas.

options: Embeddings work best on focused passages and context windows are finite
Vector databases reject documents over 10 words
It converts text into tokens automatically
questions.text: Why is chunking required before embedding documents, per the lesson?

options: It splits strictly every 1000 characters regardless of content
It breaks on paragraphs, then lines, then words
It splits only on sentence-ending punctuation
It splits by model tokens exclusively
questions.text: How does RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter try to keep chunks coherent?

options: To carry over context so an idea is not cut in half between chunks
To limit the number of chunks created

lessons.title: Embeddings and Vector Representations
lessons.content:
🧮 Embeddings and Vector Representations

📘 Lesson Overview: Embeddings turn text into vectors so that similar meanings are close in space. They are the bridge between your documents and semantic search. This lesson shows how to generate embeddings in LangChain and measure similarity.
Learning Objectives
  • Generate embeddings with OpenAI and Hugging Face.
  • Use embed_query vs embed_documents.
  • Measure similarity between texts.
  • Choose an embedding model.

1 — Generating Embeddings

from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
emb = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

v = emb.embed_query("LangChain orchestrates LLMs.")
print(len(v))                       # vector length, e.g. 1536
vs = emb.embed_documents(["doc one", "doc two"])   # list of vectors
Concept

Use embed_query for a single search query and embed_documents for the corpus you store. Both must use the same model — vectors from different models are not comparable.

2 — Similarity

Closeness is the cosine of the angle between vectors. Higher = more similar in meaning.

"dog" ~ "puppy"
0.89
"dog" ~ "cat"
0.68
"dog" ~ "rocket"
0.21
import numpy as np
def cos(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))
print(round(cos(emb.embed_query("dog"), emb.embed_query("puppy")), 3))

3 — Choosing a Model

Cheap, fast
3-small
1536 dims
Higher quality
3-large
3072 dims
Local / free
HF
sentence-transformers
Rule: Bigger dimensions = better recall but more storage and compute. Start small; upgrade only if retrieval quality is lacking.

4 — Practical Exercises

Rank by similarity

Embed a query and three candidate sentences, then print them sorted by cosine similarity to the query.
Show Solution
import numpy as np
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
emb = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
def cos(a,b): a,b=np.array(a),np.array(b); return a@b/(np.linalg.norm(a)*np.linalg.norm(b))
q = emb.embed_query("how to train a model")
cands = ["model training tips", "best pizza recipe", "fine-tuning guide"]
ce = emb.embed_documents(cands)
for s,_ in sorted(zip(cands, ce), key=lambda p: -cos(q, p[1])): print(s)
What you learned in this lesson
  • Embeddings map text to vectors where meaning = closeness.
  • embed_query for queries, embed_documents for the corpus.
  • Cosine similarity ranks relevance.
  • Bigger models recall better but cost more storage/compute.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Embed 10 short sentences, build a similarity matrix, and cluster the two or three closest pairs.

options: embed_query for the stored corpus; embed_documents for a single query
embed_query for a single search query; embed_documents for the corpus you store
They are interchangeable and produce identical vectors
embed_query only works with Hugging Face models
questions.text: When should you use embed_query vs embed_documents, according to the lesson?
The lesson warns that vectors must share one thing to be comparable. What is it?

options: By the cosine of the angle between vectors
questions.text: How is similarity between embeddings measured in the lesson?

lessons.title: Vector Databases and Semantic Search
lessons.content:
🗄️ Vector Databases and Semantic Search

📘 Lesson Overview: A vector database stores embeddings and finds the nearest ones to a query fast — even across millions of documents. This lesson covers FAISS, Chroma, Pinecone, Weaviate, and Milvus, and how to turn any of them into a LangChain retriever.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain what a vector database does.
  • Build a store from documents.
  • Run a similarity search with k.
  • Expose it as a retriever for chains.

1 — The Options

LOCAL
FAISS
In-memory, fast, great for prototypes and small corpora.
LOCAL
ChromaDB
Persistent local store, simple API.
CLOUD
Pinecone
Managed, scales to billions of vectors.
CLOUD
Weaviate · Milvus
Open-source, self-host or managed, hybrid search.

2 — Build and Search

from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

store = FAISS.from_documents(chunks, OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))
hits = store.similarity_search("refund policy", k=3)
print(hits[0].page_content)
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

store = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"),
                              persist_directory="./db")
hits = store.similarity_search("refund policy", k=3)

k is how many nearest chunks to return. Higher k = more context but more noise and tokens.

3 — As a Retriever

Convert the store into a Runnable retriever you can pipe into a chain.

retriever = store.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4})
docs = retriever.invoke("how do I reset my password?")
StorePersistBest for
FAISSsave/load filePrototypes, small data
Chromalocal dirLocal apps
PineconemanagedProduction scale
Weaviate/Milvusself/managedHybrid + scale

4 — Practical Exercises

Index and query

Build a FAISS store from a handful of short texts and return the top 2 matches for a query.
Show Solution
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
texts = ["Reset password in settings.", "We ship worldwide.", "Refunds within 30 days."]
store = FAISS.from_texts(texts, OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small"))
for d in store.similarity_search("money back", k=2): print(d.page_content)
What you learned in this lesson
  • Vector DBs store embeddings and find nearest neighbors fast.
  • from_documents builds a store; similarity_search(k) queries it.
  • as_retriever turns it into a chain-ready Runnable.
  • FAISS/Chroma local; Pinecone/Weaviate/Milvus for scale.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Persist a Chroma store to disk, reload it in a fresh script, and confirm searches still work without re-embedding.

options: Store embeddings and find the nearest ones to a query fast

The number of retries on failure

lessons.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
lessons.content:
📚 Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

📘 Lesson Overview: RAG grounds an LLM in your documents: retrieve the most relevant chunks, stuff them into the prompt, and let the model answer from that context. It reduces hallucination and lets the model use private, up-to-date knowledge. This lesson builds an end-to-end RAG chain.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain the RAG pattern and why it helps.
  • Assemble retrieve → prompt → generate with LCEL.
  • Cite sources from metadata.
  • Know RAG vs fine-tuning trade-offs.

1 — The RAG Pattern

RAG at Query Time
Concept

RAG does not retrain the model. It changes the prompt at query time by inserting facts the model can read. Update your documents and answers update instantly — no retraining.

2 — Build the Chain

from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Answer using ONLY the context. If unknown, say so.\n\nContext:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"
)
def format_docs(docs):
    return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

rag = (
    {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
    | prompt | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini") | StrOutputParser()
)
print(rag.invoke("What is our refund window?"))
Rule: Always instruct the model to answer only from context and to admit when the answer is not present. This is the single biggest hallucination reducer in RAG.

3 — RAG vs Fine-Tuning

RAG — usually start here
  • Add/update knowledge instantly.
  • Cite sources.
  • No training cost.
Fine-tuning
  • Teaches style/format, not new facts.
  • Costly to train and refresh.
  • No built-in citations.

4 — Practical Exercises

Mini RAG

Build a FAISS store from 4-5 facts, wire the RAG chain above, and ask a question answerable only from those facts.
Show Solution
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
facts = ["Our refund window is 30 days.", "Support hours are 9-5 IST.", "We ship to 40 countries."]
retriever = FAISS.from_texts(facts, OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")).as_retriever()
# then reuse the rag chain from section 2
print(rag.invoke("How long do I have to get a refund?"))
What you learned in this lesson
  • RAG retrieves chunks and injects them into the prompt.
  • It grounds answers and cuts hallucination — no retraining.
  • LCEL wires retrieve → prompt → generate cleanly.
  • Prefer RAG for knowledge; fine-tune for style/format.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Extend the RAG chain to also return the source metadata of each retrieved chunk, so answers come with citations.

options: It retrains (fine-tunes) the model on your documents
It changes the prompt at query time by inserting retrieved facts
It replaces the model with a search engine

options: Always set k to 10 or higher
Tell the model to answer only from the context and to admit when the answer is not present

options: When you need to teach the model a new writing style

When you want to reduce the embedding dimension
When you need faster token streaming

lessons.title: Agents and Tool Calling Systems
lessons.content:
🛠️ Agents and Tool Calling Systems

📘 Lesson Overview: An agent lets the LLM decide which actions to take — calling tools like search, a calculator, or your APIs — and loop until it has an answer. This lesson explains tool calling, the reason-act loop, and how to build an agent in LangChain.
Learning Objectives
  • Define a tool the model can call.
  • Explain the reason → act → observe loop.
  • Build and run an agent.
  • Guard against runaway loops and cost.

1 — Tools

A tool is a plain function with a description. The @tool decorator exposes it to the model, which reads the description to decide when to call it.

from langchain_core.tools import tool

@tool
def word_count(text: str) -> int:
    "Return the number of words in the text."
    return len(text.split())

2 — The Reason-Act Loop

Agent Loop

The model reasons about the task, calls a tool, reads the result, and repeats until it can answer — or hits a step limit.

3 — Build an Agent

from langchain.agents import create_react_agent, AgentExecutor
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain import hub

tools = [word_count]
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
prompt = hub.pull("hwchase17/react")          # standard ReAct prompt
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools, prompt)
executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, max_iterations=5, verbose=True)
print(executor.invoke({"input": "How many words are in 'the quick brown fox'?"}))
Warning — cap the loop

Agents can loop forever or rack up cost. Always set max_iterations (and ideally a timeout). Give the model the fewest tools it needs — more tools = more confusion.

4 — Tool Calling vs Chains

UseWhen
ChainSteps are known and fixed
AgentThe model must decide which steps to take
RAGYou only need to fetch + answer
Rule: Prefer a fixed chain when you can. Agents are powerful but less predictable — reach for them only when the path truly depends on the input.

5 — Practical Exercises

Calculator agent

Define a multiply(a, b) tool and build an agent that uses it to answer "what is 23 times 17?".
Show Solution
from langchain_core.tools import tool
@tool
def multiply(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    "Multiply two integers."
    return a * b
# build the agent as in section 3 with tools=[multiply]
# executor.invoke({"input": "What is 23 times 17?"})
What you learned in this lesson
  • Tools are described functions the model can call.
  • Agents loop: reason → act → observe → answer.
  • create_react_agent + AgentExecutor run the loop.
  • Cap iterations and minimize tools to stay safe and cheap.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Give an agent two tools (a calculator and a fake "weather" function) and ask a question that needs both. Watch the loop in verbose mode.

options: Reason, act (call a tool), observe the result, and repeat until it can answer
questions.text: How does the lesson describe the agent's core loop?

options: It exposes a vector; the model uses cosine similarity
It exposes a described function; the model reads the description to decide when to call it
It exposes an API key; the model authenticates with it
It exposes a chunk; the model splits it
questions.text: What does the @tool decorator expose to the model, and how does the model decide when to call it?

options: Giving the agent as many tools as possible
questions.text: What safety measure does the lesson insist on to prevent runaway agents?

lessons.title: LangChain Tools and External Integrations
lessons.content:
🧰 LangChain Tools and External Integrations

📘 Lesson Overview: Tools are how an LLM reaches outside itself — web search, a calculator, SQL databases, HTTP APIs, scrapers. This lesson shows how to use built-in toolkits, wrap your own APIs as tools, and connect LangChain to real systems.
Learning Objectives
  • Use built-in tools and toolkits.
  • Wrap any function or API as a tool.
  • Connect a SQL database.
  • Write tool descriptions the model can use.

1 — Built-in Tools

WEB
Web Search
Tavily, SerpAPI, DuckDuckGo for live information.
DB
SQL Toolkit
Query databases in natural language.
API
Requests / API
Call REST endpoints as tools.
CODE
Python REPL
Run code for math and data tasks (sandbox it!).

2 — Wrap Your Own API

Any function becomes a tool with @tool. A clear docstring and typed args are what the model reads to call it correctly.

import requests
from langchain_core.tools import tool

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    "Get the current weather for a city by name."
    r = requests.get(f"https://wttr.in/{city}?format=3", timeout=10)
    return r.text
Rule: The docstring is the model's instruction manual. Write it like you would for a junior dev: what it does, what each arg means, what it returns.

3 — SQL in Natural Language

from langchain_community.utilities import SQLDatabase
from langchain_community.agent_toolkits import create_sql_agent
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

db = SQLDatabase.from_uri("sqlite:///sales.db")
agent = create_sql_agent(ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"), db=db, verbose=True)
agent.invoke({"input": "Total revenue by month last year?"})
Warning — read-only DB access

Give the agent a read-only database user. Without it, a wrong query could delete or modify data. Never point an autonomous agent at a writable production database.

4 — Tool Design Cheat Sheet

DoWhy
One job per toolEasier for the model to choose
Typed argsValidated, structured calls
Clear docstringDrives correct usage
Return strings/JSONModel can read the result
Handle errors insideReturn a message, do not crash

5 — Practical Exercises

API tool

Wrap a public API (e.g. a currency or jokes API) as a @tool and call it directly to verify the output.
Show Solution
import requests
from langchain_core.tools import tool
@tool
def random_joke() -> str:
    "Return a random programming joke."
    r = requests.get("https://official-joke-api.appspot.com/jokes/programming/random", timeout=10)
    j = r.json()[0]
    return f"{j['setup']} — {j['punchline']}"
print(random_joke.invoke({}))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Tools connect the LLM to search, DBs, APIs, and code.
  • @tool + a clear docstring exposes any function.
  • SQL toolkits answer questions over databases.
  • Use read-only access and handle errors inside tools.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a 2-tool agent (web search + your weather tool) that answers "what should I wear in {city} today?" using live data.

options: The model's instruction manual for what the tool does and how to call it
A place to store the API key

options: Always use a writable production user
Use a read-only database user so a wrong query cannot delete or modify data
Point the agent directly at production for accuracy
questions.text: What does the lesson warn about when giving an agent SQL database access?

options: Handle many unrelated jobs to reduce tool count
One job per tool, with typed args and a clear docstring
Return raw Python objects rather than strings/JSON
Crash on error so the agent stops

lessons.title: LangGraph and AI Agent Orchestration
lessons.content:
🕸️ LangGraph and AI Agent Orchestration

📘 Lesson Overview: Chains are linear; real agents need loops, branches, and shared state. LangGraph models an app as a graph of nodes (steps) and edges (transitions) over a typed state. This lesson builds a stateful graph and explains multi-agent orchestration.
Learning Objectives
  • Explain nodes, edges, and state in LangGraph.
  • Build a graph with a conditional edge.
  • Add cycles for agent loops.
  • Know when to use LangGraph over LCEL.

1 — Graphs over Chains

LCEL is great for fixed pipelines. When you need branching, retries, or a loop that runs until a condition is met, a graph models it more naturally.

A Simple Agent Graph
Concept

A LangGraph state is a typed dict every node reads and updates. Nodes are functions; edges decide what runs next — including loops back to a previous node.

2 — Build a Graph

from typing import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END

class State(TypedDict):
    text: str
    done: bool

def step(state: State) -> State:
    return {"text": state["text"].upper(), "done": True}

g = StateGraph(State)
g.add_node("shout", step)
g.add_edge(START, "shout")
g.add_edge("shout", END)
app = g.compile()
print(app.invoke({"text": "hi", "done": False}))

3 — Conditional Edges & Loops

A routing function returns the name of the next node, enabling branches and cycles.

def route(state: State) -> str:
    return END if state["done"] else "shout"

g.add_conditional_edges("shout", route, {"shout": "shout", END: END})
Warning — bound your loops

Cyclic graphs can run forever. Track a step counter in state and route to END after a maximum, or pass a recursion limit when invoking.

4 — When to Use What

NeedUse
Fixed pipelineLCEL chain
Branches / loops / retriesLangGraph
Multiple cooperating agentsLangGraph (multi-agent)
Human-in-the-loop approvalLangGraph (interrupts)

5 — Practical Exercises

Counter graph

Build a graph that increments a number in state and loops until it reaches 3, then ends.
Show Solution
from typing import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END
class S(TypedDict): n: int
def inc(s): return {"n": s["n"] + 1}
def route(s): return END if s["n"] >= 3 else "inc"
g = StateGraph(S); g.add_node("inc", inc); g.add_edge(START, "inc")
g.add_conditional_edges("inc", route, {"inc": "inc", END: END})
print(g.compile().invoke({"n": 0}))   # {'n': 3}
What you learned in this lesson
  • LangGraph = nodes + edges over a typed state.
  • Conditional edges enable branches and loops.
  • Use it for cyclic, multi-agent, or human-in-the-loop flows.
  • Always bound cycles to avoid infinite loops.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Build a two-node graph — a "writer" node and a "critic" node that loops back until the critic approves (or after 3 rounds).

options: A linear chain of prompts joined by the pipe
A graph of nodes (steps) and edges (transitions) over a typed state
A single Runnable with no branching
A vector store with retrieval edges

Whenever you need to stream tokens

lessons.title: Streamlit and LangChain Web Applications
lessons.content:
🎈 Streamlit and LangChain Web Applications

📘 Lesson Overview: Streamlit turns a Python script into a web app with no front-end code — perfect for shipping LangChain demos and internal tools fast. This lesson builds a streaming chatbot UI and wires in session state for memory.
Learning Objectives
  • Build a chat UI with st.chat_message.
  • Stream LLM tokens to the page.
  • Persist history in st.session_state.
  • Run the app with streamlit run.

1 — A Streaming Chatbot

import streamlit as st
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

st.title("LangChain Chatbot")
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

if "msgs" not in st.session_state:
    st.session_state.msgs = []

for m in st.session_state.msgs:                 # replay history
    st.chat_message(m["role"]).write(m["content"])

if q := st.chat_input("Ask me anything"):
    st.session_state.msgs.append({"role": "user", "content": q})
    st.chat_message("user").write(q)
    with st.chat_message("assistant"):
        answer = st.write_stream(model.stream(q))   # token streaming
    st.session_state.msgs.append({"role": "assistant", "content": answer})
Warning — the script reruns every interaction

Streamlit re-executes top to bottom on each input. Anything you must keep (history, the model, a vector store) belongs in st.session_state or a @st.cache_resource function — otherwise it is rebuilt every keystroke.

2 — Cache Expensive Resources

import streamlit as st
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

@st.cache_resource
def get_model():
    return ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

model = get_model()   # built once, reused across reruns
Rule: @st.cache_resource for connections/models (one shared instance); @st.cache_data for data results (serializable values).

3 — Run It

$ streamlit run app.py Local URL: http://localhost:8501

4 — Practical Exercises

RAG chat UI

Add a file uploader; when a PDF is uploaded, build a retriever (cached) and answer questions over it in the chat.
Show Solution
import streamlit as st
file = st.file_uploader("PDF", type="pdf")
# load -> split -> FAISS.from_documents (wrap in @st.cache_resource keyed by file)
# then: answer = st.write_stream(rag_chain.stream(question))
What you learned in this lesson
  • Streamlit ships LangChain apps with no front-end code.
  • st.chat_message + st.chat_input build a chat UI.
  • st.write_stream streams tokens live.
  • Persist with session_state; cache models with cache_resource.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Add a sidebar with a model picker and a temperature slider, and a "Clear chat" button that resets session_state.

options: It never re-runs the script once started
It re-executes the script top to bottom on every interaction, so state must live in session_state or a cached function
It runs each function in a separate process
questions.text: What does the lesson warn about Streamlit's execution model?
Which decorator does the lesson recommend for expensive resources like models or connections?

options: Saves the chat history to disk
Streams the model's tokens live to the page
questions.text: What does st.write_stream do in the chatbot example?

lessons.title: FastAPI and Backend AI Development
lessons.content:
⚡ FastAPI and Backend AI Development

📘 Lesson Overview: To serve a LangChain app to other systems you expose it as an HTTP API. FastAPI is the standard: async, typed, auto-documented. This lesson builds an endpoint that runs a chain, streams responses, and handles errors.
Learning Objectives
  • Expose a chain via a FastAPI endpoint.
  • Validate requests with Pydantic.
  • Stream responses to clients.
  • Handle errors and keys safely.

1 — A Chain Endpoint

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

app = FastAPI()
chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Answer: {q}") | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini") | StrOutputParser()

class Ask(BaseModel):
    q: str

@app.post("/ask")
async def ask(body: Ask):
    return {"answer": await chain.ainvoke({"q": body.q})}
Rule: Use async def + ainvoke/astream in FastAPI so one slow LLM call does not block other requests.

2 — Streaming Endpoint

from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

@app.post("/stream")
async def stream(body: Ask):
    async def gen():
        async for chunk in chain.astream({"q": body.q}):
            yield chunk
    return StreamingResponse(gen(), media_type="text/plain")

3 — Run & Docs

Install

pip install fastapi uvicorn

Run

uvicorn app:app --reload

Explore

Open /docs for auto-generated Swagger UI.

Warning — never expose keys to clients

Keys live on the server in env vars. The browser/client only talks to your API, never the LLM provider directly. Add auth (API key header or JWT) before going public.

4 — Streamlit vs FastAPI

FastAPI
  • Headless API for any client.
  • Async, scalable, typed.
  • Auth, versioning, microservices.
Streamlit
  • Instant UI, no front-end code.
  • Great for demos and internal tools.
  • Not a general-purpose API.

5 — Practical Exercises

Health + ask

Add a GET /health returning {"ok": true} and test the /ask endpoint with curl.
Show Solution
@app.get("/health")
def health():
    return {"ok": True}
# curl -X POST localhost:8000/ask -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"q":"hi"}'
What you learned in this lesson
  • FastAPI serves chains as HTTP endpoints.
  • Pydantic validates request bodies.
  • Use async + astream for non-blocking streaming.
  • Keep keys server-side; add auth before public release.
🏋️ Take-Home Exercise: Add an API-key header dependency that rejects requests without a valid key, and return a 401 otherwise.

options: So keys can be exposed to the client
So one slow LLM call does not block other requests
So the model retrains between calls
So you can skip Pydantic validation

What validates request bodies in the lesson's FastAPI example?

options: They should be sent to the client for direct provider calls
They live on the server in env vars; the client only talks to your API, and you should add auth before going public
They must be baked into the Docker image
They belong in the Pydantic request model
